title: Think Before You Decide — Reasoning Under Uncertainty
description: Your gut is wrong more often than you think. Learn the cognitive biases that distort your decisions and the frameworks that correct them.

sequences.title: Base Rates
sequences.description: Why your gut is wrong about probability — and why dramatic stories beat statistics every time.

content.body:
Here's a puzzle.

A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people. You take a test that's 99% accurate. The test comes back positive.

What's the probability you actually have the disease?

Most people say 99%. The real answer is about 9%.

If that surprises you, you just experienced **base rate neglect** — one of the most common and most consequential errors in human reasoning. Your brain ignored the background frequency (1 in 1,000) and fixated on the test result (99% accurate).

This sequence is about learning to think in base rates — the background probabilities that should anchor your judgments but almost never do.

## Why 9%?

Let's walk through it with real numbers.

Imagine 1,000 people take the test.

**1 person** actually has the disease. The test correctly identifies them. That's 1 true positive.

**999 people** don't have the disease. But the test is only 99% accurate, so it incorrectly flags 1% of healthy people. That's about **10 false positives.**

So you have 11 total positive results — but only 1 of them is real. That's 1 out of 11, or roughly 9%.

The key insight: when the base rate of something is very low, even a highly accurate test produces more false positives than true positives. The test is good. But the math of rare events overwhelms the test's accuracy.

This isn't just about medical tests. It applies to any situation where you're trying to detect something rare: fraud detection, hiring decisions, security screening, criminal profiling. Whenever the thing you're looking for is uncommon, false alarms will dominate — and your gut will consistently overestimate the real rate.

content.prompt:
A company implements an AI system to flag fraudulent transactions. The system is 95% accurate. Fraud occurs in 0.1% of all transactions.

Out of 100,000 transactions, roughly how many flagged transactions will actually be fraudulent?

options.text: About 95% of flagged transactions will be real fraud.
options.explanation: This is the base rate neglect trap. You're applying the test accuracy (95%) directly to the flagged results. But fraud is rare — 0.1% — so you need to work through the actual numbers. Out of 100,000 transactions, 100 are fraud (the system catches 95). The other 99,900 are legitimate, but 5% get falsely flagged — that's about 4,995 false alarms. So 95 real catches out of roughly 5,090 total flags. Only about 1.9% of flags are real fraud.

options.text: Less than 2% — the false alarms will vastly outnumber real fraud because fraud is so rare.
options.explanation: Right. About 95 real fraud cases caught, but roughly 4,995 false alarms — so only about 1.9% of flagged transactions are actually fraudulent. The system is 95% accurate, but 98% of its alerts are wrong. This is why fraud teams spend most of their time reviewing false positives.

options.text: About 50% — some fraud will be missed and some legitimate transactions will be flagged.
options.explanation: This splits the difference, which feels intuitively fair but isn't how the math works. The ratio depends entirely on the base rate. When the thing you're looking for is rare (0.1%), even a good detector produces overwhelmingly more false positives than true positives.

content.body:
## Base Rates in Everyday Life

Base rate neglect isn't just a math problem. It shapes how you see the world every day.

**News:** A plane crash kills 200 people and dominates the news for a week. You feel anxious about your upcoming flight. But the base rate of dying in a plane crash is about 1 in 11 million. You drove to the airport — a trip with a death rate roughly 100 times higher — without a second thought.

**Hiring:** A candidate went to a prestigious school. You assume they're excellent. But the base rate of excellent performers from any school is a distribution, not a guarantee.

**Crime:** A neighborhood is described as "dangerous." You avoid it. But the base rate of actually being victimized on any given visit might be vanishingly small. The label substitutes for the probability.

In each case, a vivid story, credential, or label overrides the actual frequency. Your brain processes the salient detail and skips the denominator.

content.prompt:
**This one is designed to fool you.**

You're evaluating two sales candidates.

Candidate A: Top performer at their last company. Glowing reference.

Candidate B: Decent track record. Solid reference.

You have data showing that only 10% of new hires in this role become top performers, regardless of previous track record.

Which candidate is more likely to become a top performer at your company?

options.text: Candidate A — their track record is strong evidence of future performance.
options.explanation: This feels obvious, and Candidate A may have a somewhat higher probability. But the base rate is 10% for top performance regardless of background. Research consistently shows that past performance in a different context is a much weaker predictor than people assume. You're weighting the vivid story over the base rate.

options.text: Neither is very likely — the base rate of top performance is 10%, and individual signals are weaker predictors than we think.
options.explanation: The base rate anchors the estimate. Candidate A's track record might push their probability to 15-20%, but it doesn't make them a likely top performer. Hiring decisions improve dramatically when you respect the base rate instead of being dazzled by standout stories.

options.text: You can't know — the base rate is irrelevant to individual cases.
options.explanation: The base rate isn't irrelevant — it's your starting point. Without other information, each candidate has a 10% chance. New evidence should update that estimate modestly, not dramatically. Dismissing the base rate is exactly how base rate neglect works.

content.body:
## Why We Ignore Base Rates

The human brain has two ways of processing probability:

**System 1** (fast, automatic): responds to stories, images, emotions, and vivid details. When you hear about a shark attack, System 1 updates your fear of sharks immediately.

**System 2** (slow, deliberate): works with numbers, statistics, and abstract reasoning. System 2 knows that vending machines kill more people than sharks.

Base rates are System 2 information — abstract, statistical, boring. Individual cases are System 1 information — vivid, emotional, memorable. When the two conflict, System 1 almost always wins.

The fix isn't to eliminate System 1. It's to learn when to pause and ask: *what's the base rate?*

content.prompt:
Think of a decision you made — or a belief you hold — where you might be ignoring the base rate.

Describe the situation and estimate what the base rate might actually be. How does that change your assessment?
content.sampleAnswer: Common examples: overestimating the risk of rare but vivid events (terrorism, plane crashes), underestimating the failure rate of new businesses, or making assumptions about groups based on memorable individual examples. Even a rough base rate estimate can dramatically recalibrate your judgment.

content.body:
## What You Learned

**Base rate neglect** is the tendency to ignore background frequencies when evaluating individual cases. Your brain prefers vivid stories over abstract statistics — even when the statistics are more informative.

The defense is a single question: **What's the base rate?**

Before you evaluate a test result, ask how common the condition is. Before you evaluate a candidate, ask how often anyone succeeds in that role. Before you fear a risk, ask how frequently it actually occurs.

You don't need to become a statistician. You just need to remember the denominator.

sequences.title: Sunk Costs
sequences.description: Why 'I've already invested so much' is a trap — and why walking away is sometimes the smartest move.

content.body:
You're 90 minutes into a terrible movie. Your friend suggests leaving.

You say: "We already paid for the tickets. We might as well finish it."

Congratulations — you just fell for the **sunk cost fallacy**. The money is gone whether you stay or leave. The only question that matters is: would you rather spend the next hour watching something you're not enjoying, or doing literally anything else?

The sunk cost fallacy is the tendency to continue investing in something because of what you've already invested, rather than evaluating the decision based on future value alone. It scales from movie tickets to billion-dollar projects.

## Why It Feels Rational

The sunk cost fallacy persists because it doesn't feel like a fallacy. It feels like consistency, commitment, and responsibility.

"I've put three years into this degree — I can't quit now." But if the degree won't lead where you want to go, those three years are spent regardless. The question is whether the *remaining* years are worth it.

"We've invested $2 million in this project — we need to see it through." But if the project is failing, the $2 million is gone either way. The question is whether *additional* investment will produce returns.

In each case, the past investment creates an emotional anchor that distorts the forward-looking decision. The bigger the investment, the stronger the anchor.

content.prompt:
A startup has spent 18 months and $500,000 building a product. Early user testing shows minimal interest. The CEO says:

> *"We're too far in to pivot now. Let's push through to launch."*

A board member says:

> *"The $500K is spent either way. Would we invest the next $200K in this product if we were starting from scratch today?"*

Who's thinking more clearly?

options.text: The CEO — you can't judge a product before launch.
options.explanation: The 18 months are already spent — they're gone whether the company pivots or launches. The CEO's reasoning feels responsible but is anchored to past investment rather than future prospects.

options.text: The board member — they're correctly separating past costs from forward-looking decisions.
options.explanation: The board member's question is the antidote to sunk cost thinking: "Would we make this investment if we were starting fresh?" If the answer is no, then the only reason to continue is emotional attachment to the money already spent.

options.text: Neither — you'd need more data before deciding.
options.explanation: More data might be useful, but the question is about reasoning structure. The CEO and the board member have the same data. The board member's framework — evaluate future decisions based on future value — is structurally sounder regardless of what additional data might show.

content.body:
## The Emotional Machinery

Sunk cost thinking is powered by two emotions:

**Loss aversion.** Humans feel losses about twice as intensely as equivalent gains. Abandoning a $500K investment *feels* like losing $500K, even though the money is gone either way.

**Identity protection.** Admitting that a past decision was wrong threatens your self-image. Continuing the investment lets you postpone that reckoning.

These emotions don't make you irrational — they make you human. But they create a systematic bias toward continuing failing endeavors. The bigger and more public the investment, the harder it becomes to walk away.

This is why governments finish bridges to nowhere, companies ship products nobody wants, and people stay in careers that make them miserable.

content.prompt:
**This one is designed to fool you.**

You've been studying for a certification for 6 months. A colleague tells you it's become much less valued by employers.

What should you do?

options.text: Finish — you're halfway done, and having it can't hurt.
options.explanation: "Can't hurt" is doing a lot of work here. The remaining 6 months have a real cost — time you could spend on something more valuable. If you wouldn't start this certification today knowing what you know now, why would you continue it?

options.text: Investigate the claim. If true, redirect your remaining study time toward something more valuable.
options.explanation: Verify first, then decide based on forward-looking value. If the certification has declining value, the correct calculation is: "What's the best use of my next 6 months?" not "How do I justify my last 6 months?"

options.text: Quit immediately.
options.explanation: One colleague's opinion isn't sufficient evidence. The right move is to investigate, then decide. Quitting on one data point is just as unreflective as continuing out of sunk cost attachment.

content.body:
## The Kill Question

Professional investors use a simple mental tool to defeat sunk cost thinking:

**"Knowing what I know now, would I start this today?"**

If the answer is no — if you wouldn't begin this project, this relationship, this career with today's knowledge — then the only reason you're continuing is because you already started. And "because I already started" is not a reason. It's an anchor.

The kill question works because it strips away the past investment entirely. It forces you to evaluate the decision on its forward-looking merits alone.

The hardest part isn't asking the question. It's being honest with the answer.

content.prompt:
Apply the kill question to something in your own life — a project, a commitment, a habit, a plan.

"Knowing what I know now, would I start this today?"

If yes, good. If no, what's actually keeping you in?
content.sampleAnswer: Many people discover they're continuing something primarily because of past investment. Note: the kill question doesn't always mean you should quit. Sometimes switching costs are real. The point is to make the decision for the right reasons.

content.body:
## What You Learned

The **sunk cost fallacy** is continuing to invest in something because of what you've already put in, rather than because of what you expect to get out.

It's powered by loss aversion and identity protection. These emotions scale with the size of the investment.

The defense is the **kill question**: "Knowing what I know now, would I start this today?"

Past costs are gone. The only costs that matter are future ones.

sequences.title: Confirmation Bias
sequences.description: Why you only see what you already believe — and how to look for what you're missing.

content.body:
You think your new coworker is incompetent. Over the next week, you notice every mistake they make — a typo, a naive question, a missed deadline. Each observation confirms what you already believed.

But here's what you didn't notice: two solid presentations, a creative solution to a client problem, and a thank-you email from their team lead. That evidence was there. You just didn't see it.

This is **confirmation bias** — the tendency to seek, notice, interpret, and remember information that confirms what you already believe, while ignoring information that contradicts it.

Confirmation bias isn't about stupidity. Intelligent people are often *more* susceptible, because they're better at constructing arguments that support their existing views.

## The Three Stages

Confirmation bias operates at three levels:

**1. Selective search.** You look for evidence that supports your view and don't look for evidence that challenges it. If you think a restaurant is overrated, you scan for negative reviews.

**2. Selective interpretation.** Ambiguous evidence gets interpreted in the direction of your existing belief. A politician you like gives a vague answer — you hear nuance. A politician you dislike gives the same answer — you hear evasion.

**3. Selective memory.** You remember confirming evidence more readily than contradicting evidence. After an argument, both people remember the moments that support their side.

At each stage, your information environment becomes more biased — and the bias is invisible because it feels like thorough, honest evaluation.

content.prompt:
A manager believes remote work reduces productivity. They commission a study showing:

- Remote workers complete 5% fewer tasks per week
- Remote workers produce 12% fewer errors
- Remote workers report higher satisfaction
- Remote workers have 20% lower turnover

The manager concludes: "The data confirms remote work hurts productivity."

What happened?

options.text: The manager is correct — 5% fewer tasks is a genuine productivity decrease.
options.explanation: The 5% task reduction is real, but it's one data point in a set of four. Fewer errors, higher satisfaction, and lower turnover all have productivity implications too. The manager selected the one metric that confirmed their prior belief and ignored three that complicated it.

options.text: Confirmation bias — the manager focused on the one supporting data point and discounted three that challenge their belief.
options.explanation: The data is mixed, not confirmatory. A balanced reading would note tradeoffs worth examining. Instead, the manager's prior belief filtered the results: the confirming point became "the conclusion" while the disconfirming points became noise.

options.text: The study is flawed — you can't measure productivity with just four metrics.
options.explanation: Attacking the methodology misses the point. The issue is how the manager *read* the results. Four metrics were presented; one supported their view; they cited only the one. The problem isn't the data — it's the filter.

content.body:
## Why It's So Hard to Fix

Your brain is a prediction machine. It builds models and processes new information through them. Confirming information slots in easily. Disconfirming information creates friction.

Given a choice between "slot it in" and "do cognitive work," your brain almost always chooses easy. Not because you're lazy, but because processing every piece of information from scratch would be paralyzing.

The uncomfortable implication: **you cannot trust your sense that the evidence supports your view.** Not because you're dishonest, but because your evidence-gathering, evidence-interpreting, and evidence-remembering systems are all biased toward confirming what you started with.

content.prompt:
**This one is designed to fool you.**

You've done extensive research on buying a house. Dozens of articles. Talked to homeowners. Analyzed your finances. You feel confident.

A friend asks: "How many articles did you read arguing that renting might be better?"

The answer is zero.

What's the most honest assessment of your research?

options.text: Thorough — you read dozens of articles and talked to real people.
options.explanation: Volume isn't balance. Dozens of articles all supporting the same conclusion isn't research — it's reinforcement. The absence of counter-evidence isn't because there isn't any.

options.text: One-sided — you gathered evidence supporting a decision you'd already made, not evidence to test it.
options.explanation: Reading extensively about why buying is good isn't the same as evaluating whether buying is right. If you can't state the strongest argument for renting in your situation, you haven't evaluated the decision. You've confirmed it.

options.text: It doesn't matter — reading opposing views would just be contrarianism.
options.explanation: This is what confirmation bias sounds like from the inside. Looking at opposing evidence isn't contrarianism — it's due diligence.

content.body:
## The Counter-Practice

You can't eliminate confirmation bias. But you can counteract it.

**1. Seek disconfirmation deliberately.** Ask: "What evidence would change my mind?" Then go look for it.

**2. Steel-man the opposition.** Construct the strongest possible argument against your position. Not a strawman — a genuine, charitable version.

**3. Check your sources.** If every piece of evidence points the same direction, that's a red flag, not confirmation. Reality is rarely that clean.

**4. Ask a disagreeing friend.** Their evidence is the evidence you're systematically missing.

content.prompt:
Pick a belief you hold with high confidence.

Now construct the strongest possible counter-argument — the version a smart, well-informed person who disagrees with you would actually make.

How does your confidence change?
content.sampleAnswer: If the exercise was easy, you either picked a trivial belief or didn't engage with the counter-argument. Most people find their confidence drops slightly, which is appropriate — it means the original confidence was partly inflated by confirmation bias.

content.body:
## What You Learned

**Confirmation bias** is the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms existing beliefs. It operates at every stage — search, interpretation, memory — and gets stronger with intelligence.

You cannot eliminate it. But you can counteract it by seeking disconfirmation, steel-manning opposing views, checking source diversity, and consulting disagreers.

The test isn't whether you can defend your position. It's whether you can articulate the strongest argument against it.

sequences.title: Availability Bias
sequences.description: Why dramatic events distort your sense of risk — and why the boring dangers are the real ones.

content.body:
Quick: which kills more people — shark attacks or falling coconuts?

If you said sharks, you're wrong. Falling coconuts kill roughly 150 people per year worldwide. Sharks kill about 10. But almost no one is afraid of coconut trees.

This is **availability bias** — the tendency to judge the frequency or probability of an event based on how easily examples come to mind. Shark attacks are vivid, dramatic, and heavily covered by media. Coconut deaths are mundane and unreported. So your brain codes sharks as dangerous and coconuts as harmless, even though the numbers say the opposite.

## The Mechanism

Your brain estimates frequency using a shortcut: **if examples come to mind quickly, the thing must be common.**

This works for things you encounter directly. If you recall many times getting stuck on Route 9, Route 9 probably does have bad traffic.

It breaks down when your "sample" comes from media. News reports plane crashes but not car crashes. Social media amplifies outrage but not contentment. Crime dramas depict murders but not property crimes.

The result: your mental map of "what's common" is systematically distorted toward the vivid and dramatic — and away from the mundane and statistical.

Heart disease kills 700,000 Americans per year. Terrorism kills fewer than 100. But ask people what they're afraid of, and the numbers invert. That inversion is availability bias.

content.prompt:
After reading several articles about startup founders becoming millionaires, you're considering quitting your job to launch a startup.

Which bias is most likely influencing your decision?

options.text: Confirmation bias — you're only reading success stories.
options.explanation: Confirmation bias might be secondary, but the primary driver is availability. The articles made startup success vivid and mentally accessible. The failures — which vastly outnumber successes — simply aren't in your mental sample.

options.text: Availability bias — vivid success stories make startup success feel more common than it is.
options.explanation: Startup success stories are dramatic and heavily reported. Failures are quiet and unreported. Your brain builds its probability estimate from available examples, which are overwhelmingly skewed toward survivors.

options.text: No bias — reading about successful founders is legitimate research.
options.explanation: Reading success stories is like estimating a drug's safety by only talking to people it helped. Legitimate research would include failure rates, median outcomes, and unfiltered data.

content.body:
## Availability Cascades

Availability bias creates feedback loops in society:

1. A vivid event occurs.
2. Media covers it extensively.
3. Coverage makes the event highly available in everyone's minds.
4. People overestimate frequency.
5. Overestimation generates public concern.
6. Concern generates more coverage.
7. Policy gets made based on distorted risk perception.

This explains why public concern about risks often has little correlation with actual risk levels. The risks that dominate discourse are the most vivid and mediagenic — not the most statistically significant.

content.prompt:
**This one is designed to fool you.**

You're reviewing your team's performance. Two employees come to mind immediately: Alex, who made a costly mistake last month, and Jordan, who landed a major client last quarter.

What's the problem with rating Alex below average and Jordan above average based on these examples?

options.text: Nothing — a costly mistake and a big win are relevant data.
options.explanation: They're relevant but not representative. What about Alex's other 11 months? Jordan's day-to-day work? You're building a rating from the two most vivid memories, not a systematic review.

options.text: You're letting the most vivid events stand in for a full year. Dramatic moments are overweighted because they're most available.
options.explanation: A costly mistake and a big win are memorable because they're unusual. Performance reviews should assess typical contribution. Without systematic tracking, the most dramatic memories become the entire basis for evaluation.

options.text: You should rely on metrics instead — data doesn't lie.
options.explanation: Metrics help but don't eliminate availability bias. Which metrics you look at and which anomalies you notice are influenced by what's already available in memory.

content.prompt:
Think about something you consider dangerous or risky.

Is your sense of this risk based on statistical frequency, or on how easily you can picture it happening? Can you name specific vivid examples driving your fear (a news story, a movie scene)?

How might the real probability differ from your feeling?
content.sampleAnswer: Common examples: fear of flying, fear of violent crime, fear of rare diseases. The exercise reveals the gap between felt risk and actual risk — and traces that gap to available vivid examples rather than data.

content.body:
## The Correction

**When you feel afraid**, ask: "What's the actual rate?"

**When you feel certain about a frequency**, ask: "Am I estimating from data or from examples I can recall?"

**When a risk dominates public conversation**, ask: "Is this the biggest problem, or just the most visible one?"

Availability bias doesn't go away. But awareness gives you a crucial pause between "I can easily imagine this" and "therefore it must be common."

## What You Learned

**Availability bias** is estimating frequency based on how easily examples come to mind. Vivid and dramatic events are overweighted; mundane and statistical events are underweighted.

The defense: check intuitions against data. When afraid of something, ask how often it actually happens. When certain about a frequency, ask whether your estimate comes from numbers or memorable stories.

The boring dangers are the real ones. The dramatic ones just have better PR.

sequences.title: Anchoring
sequences.description: Why the first number you hear controls the conversation — and how to break free.

content.body:
Two groups estimate Chicago's population.

Group A is first asked: "More or less than 200,000?" Group B: "More or less than 5 million?"

Both groups know the question is arbitrary. Both dismiss it as irrelevant. And both groups' estimates are massively pulled toward the number they heard.

This is **anchoring** — the tendency for an initial piece of information to disproportionately influence subsequent judgments, even when the initial information is arbitrary or deliberately manipulative.

## Why Anchoring Works

When you don't know an answer, you start from whatever reference point is available and adjust. The problem: adjustments are almost always **insufficient** — you move away from the anchor, but not far enough.

The anchor functions as a gravitational center. The further your estimate needs to be, the more cognitive effort it takes. Your brain runs out of motivation before reaching the right answer.

This is why anchoring works even when you know about it. Knowing a number is an anchor doesn't prevent it from pulling your estimate.

Negotiators exploit this ruthlessly. The first number on the table becomes the center around which all discussion revolves. Whoever sets the anchor controls the range.

content.prompt:
You're buying a used car listed at $18,000. Similar cars sell for $13,000-$15,000. You offer $12,000. The seller counters at $16,500.

What's the anchoring dynamic?

options.text: The $18,000 listing price is the anchor — all subsequent numbers orbit around it.
options.explanation: The listing set the anchor before negotiation started. The seller's counter of $16,500 — above market — feels like a concession from $18,000 rather than an overprice above $15,000. That reframing is the anchor's power.

options.text: Your $12,000 offer is the anchor — you're pulling the price down.
options.explanation: Your offer is a counter-anchor, but the seller's price was established first. The initial anchor is stronger. You're negotiating away from $18,000, not up from $12,000.

options.text: No anchoring — both sides know the market value.
options.explanation: If both sides were purely rational, they'd converge on market value quickly. But the inflated starting point pulls the final price upward. Even knowing market value, you're influenced by the anchor.

content.body:
## Anchoring Beyond Negotiation

**Sentencing.** Judges give longer sentences when prosecutors request higher ones — even when judges call the request unreasonable.

**Fundraising.** Donation forms with suggested amounts ($50, $100, $250) produce higher average donations than open-ended forms.

**Salary.** Your current salary anchors your next one. Employers ask "What are you making now?" specifically to set a low anchor.

**Estimates.** When your boss asks "Can you finish by Friday?" your estimate is anchored to Friday — even if the work takes two weeks.

In each case, the first number sets the gravitational center.

content.prompt:
**This one is designed to fool you.**

Your boss asks: "This should take about two weeks, right?"

You hadn't estimated yet, but two weeks feels about right — maybe three to be safe.

The project takes six weeks. What happened?

options.text: Normal estimation error — the project was more complex than expected.
options.explanation: Normal error doesn't explain why your estimate clustered around your boss's suggestion. The "two weeks" anchor pulled your estimate down, and your "safe" addition of one week was insufficient adjustment.

options.text: Your boss's 'two weeks' anchored your estimate. Your 'three weeks' was insufficient adjustment from an anchor that was already too low.
options.explanation: The boss planted the anchor. Your estimate of three weeks felt cautious because it was 50% more than the anchor. But the anchor was wrong, so your adjustment was still trapped in its gravitational field.

options.text: You were trying to please your boss by agreeing with their timeline.
options.explanation: Social pressure might play a role, but anchoring happens even without it. In experiments, people's estimates are pulled toward random numbers on a spinner wheel — no boss, no social pressure. The effect is cognitive, not social.

content.body:
## Anchor Defense

**Generate your own estimate first.** Before hearing anyone else's number, write down your own.

**Consider the opposite.** Ask: "What if the answer is very different from this number?"

**Delay your response.** Anchors are strongest when you respond immediately.

**Name it.** In negotiations, say: "That number seems high — I'd rather start from market data." Explicitly rejecting an anchor weakens its pull.

**Ask about the range.** Ranges activate different cognitive processes than point estimates and are harder to anchor.

content.prompt:
Think of a negotiation or estimation where anchoring played a role — a salary discussion, a purchase, a project timeline.

What was the anchor? Where would your estimate have landed without it? What would you do differently?
content.sampleAnswer: Common examples include accepting first salary offers as baselines, using sticker prices as reference points, or accepting timelines suggested by others. The exercise reveals how often initial numbers control subsequent decisions.

content.body:
## What You Learned

**Anchoring** is the tendency for the first number you encounter to disproportionately influence your estimates, even when arbitrary or manipulative.

It works because adjustments from reference points are almost always insufficient.

The defense: generate your own estimates first, consider why the anchor might be wrong, take time before responding, and explicitly reject strategic anchors.

The first number wins. Make sure it's yours.

sequences.title: The Planning Fallacy
sequences.description: Why everything takes longer than you think — and why knowing this doesn't fix it.

content.body:
The Sydney Opera House was estimated at 4 years and $7 million. It took 16 years and $102 million.

Boston's Big Dig was budgeted at $2.8 billion. Final cost: $14.6 billion.

These aren't anomalies. They're the norm. Research shows humans systematically underestimate time, cost, and risk. Not by a little — by a lot.

This is the **planning fallacy** — optimistic predictions about future actions, even with direct experience of similar overruns.

The strangest part: knowing about it doesn't fix it. People who understand the bias still underestimate their own projects.

## Inside View vs. Outside View

The **inside view** thinks about specific steps and imagines them going well. It feels thorough but is always optimistic. You don't imagine the sick day, the dependency delay, or the three rounds of feedback instead of one.

The **outside view** asks: "How long do projects like this typically take?" It treats your project as one member of a category.

The inside view says: "I'll outline Monday, draft Tuesday-Wednesday, review Thursday, finalize Friday. One week."

The outside view says: "My last three reports took 2-3 weeks. This one will too."

The outside view is almost always more accurate. But the inside view is almost always what people use — because it feels like real planning.

content.prompt:
You estimate a bathroom renovation at 11 days based on a detailed step-by-step breakdown. Your contractor friend says: "Bathroom renovations always take 4-6 weeks."

Who should you believe?

options.text: Your estimate — it accounts for each step.
options.explanation: Your breakdown is the inside view — thorough-looking but systematically optimistic. You estimated each step going smoothly. You didn't account for backordered tile, hidden water damage, or the plumber's schedule.

options.text: Your contractor friend — their estimate is based on how long these actually take (the outside view).
options.explanation: Your friend uses the outside view: dozens of bathroom renovations, 4-6 weeks each. The detailed breakdown gives false precision. The category-based estimate gives rough accuracy. Rough accuracy beats precise optimism.

options.text: Split the difference.
options.explanation: The errors aren't symmetric. Inside view estimates are systematically low. The "middle" is still below likely reality. When inside and outside views conflict, the outside view is almost always closer.

content.body:
## Why Knowing Doesn't Help

You can tell people "you will underestimate this" and they still underestimate it. Why?

The inside view feels like information; the outside view feels like giving up. Your step-by-step simulation feels real and specific. "Projects like this take X" feels vague.

There's also motivation: optimistic timelines get projects approved and feel energizing. Realistic timelines feel discouraging.

And you always believe your project is different. "Yes, renovations usually take 6 weeks — but I've planned mine carefully." Every project's owner feels that way.

content.prompt:
A software team estimates 3 weeks. Their manager adds 50% buffer: 4.5 weeks.

The feature takes 7 weeks. What went wrong?

options.text: The buffer wasn't big enough.
options.explanation: Adding a multiplier to a biased estimate inherits the bias. If the base is 50% too low, doubling might work. If it's 70% too low, it doesn't. Multipliers don't fix bad starting points.

options.text: The buffer was applied to an inside-view estimate. They should have used the outside view: how long do similar features actually take?
options.explanation: A percentage adjustment to a systematically biased number is still biased. The outside view would ask: "How long have our last ten similar features taken?" That's the estimate — regardless of what the step-by-step plan says.

options.text: Nothing — sometimes projects just take longer.
options.explanation: "Sometimes projects take longer" is the planning fallacy talking. If similar features routinely take 6-8 weeks, then 7 weeks isn't a surprise — it's the base rate.

content.body:
## Reference Class Forecasting

The most effective counter is **reference class forecasting**:

Step 1: What category does your project belong to?
Step 2: How long do these typically take? What's the range?
Step 3: Is there a genuine reason yours will differ? ("I planned carefully" isn't a reason.)
Step 4: Use the reference class estimate.

This approach produces dramatically more accurate estimates across domains. It feels less satisfying than detailed planning because it ignores your specific plan. But it works because it incorporates the delays that detailed plans consistently miss.

content.prompt:
Think of a recent task that took significantly longer than expected.

Describe your original estimate (inside view). Then describe what actually happened. Finally: if you'd used the outside view ("how long do things like this typically take for me?"), what would your estimate have been?
content.sampleAnswer: Most people find their outside view would have been significantly more accurate. The complications feel unique but occur with remarkable regularity. The specific surprises are unpredictable; the existence of surprises is perfectly predictable.

content.body:
## What You Learned

The **planning fallacy** is systematically underestimating time, cost, and risk — even with direct experience of similar underestimates.

The inside view feels rigorous but is always optimistic. The outside view feels vague but is consistently more accurate.

The defense is **reference class forecasting**: find out how long this category of project actually takes. Adjust modestly for specifics. Resist believing your project is special.

Everything takes longer than you think. Plan accordingly.

sequences.title: Risk vs. Uncertainty
sequences.description: When you can calculate the odds and when you can't — and why the difference changes everything.

content.body:
**Risk** is when you don't know what will happen, but you know the probabilities. A coin flip is risk. A roulette wheel is risk.

**Uncertainty** is when you don't know what will happen *and* you don't know the probabilities. Starting a business is uncertainty. A pandemic is uncertainty.

This distinction changes everything about how you should decide. The tools that work for risk (expected value, probability weighting) break down under uncertainty. And most important decisions happen under uncertainty, not risk.

## Why People Confuse Them

We treat uncertainty like risk because risk is comforting. If you can assign a probability, you can manage it.

So what do people do facing genuine uncertainty? They invent probabilities.

"I'd say there's a 70% chance this startup succeeds." Based on what? There's no actuarial table for your specific business.

"There's probably a 50% chance they'll say yes." 50% is the numerical equivalent of a shrug dressed up as an estimate.

The danger: false confidence in predictions. You optimize for outcomes you can't actually predict. And when things go differently, you feel blindsided by something that was never predictable.

content.prompt:
A friend says about a job at a startup:

> *"Startups have a 90% failure rate, so there's only a 10% chance this works out."*

What's wrong with this reasoning?

options.text: The 90% rate is exaggerated.
options.explanation: The number might be roughly accurate. But that's not the problem. The problem is applying a population average to a specific case under uncertainty.

options.text: It confuses risk with uncertainty. The 90% is a population average that can't be meaningfully applied to a specific startup with unique characteristics.
options.explanation: The 90% is useful context — a base rate. But treating it as a precise probability for this specific startup treats uncertainty as risk. Some startups have much higher chances, others much lower, and you can't reliably calculate which.

options.text: The failure rate doesn't matter — follow your passion.
options.explanation: Ignoring evidence isn't better than misapplying it. The base rate is relevant input. The error is in treating a category average as a precise individual probability.

content.body:
## Decision-Making Under Uncertainty

If you can't calculate probabilities, use different tools:

**Robustness over optimization.** Choose paths that work reasonably well across many scenarios, not perfectly in one.

**Reversibility.** Prefer decisions you can undo. Under uncertainty, the ability to change course is more valuable than the ability to predict.

**Optionality.** Choose paths that keep future options open. Flexibility's value increases when you can't predict which options you'll need.

**Small experiments.** Instead of betting big on uncertain outcomes, test small. Prototypes, pilots, temporary positions. Small experiments convert uncertainty into information at low cost.

content.prompt:
Two career paths:

**A:** High salary, narrow specialization, one employer likely for a decade.
**B:** Moderate salary, broad skills, applicable across many industries.

You can't predict which industries will grow or shrink.

Using an uncertainty mindset, which is stronger?

options.text: Path A — maximize income since the future is unpredictable anyway.
options.explanation: If the future is unpredictable, you want flexibility. Path A optimizes for one scenario. If it holds, you win. If it doesn't, you're trapped.

options.text: Path B — under uncertainty, optionality and adaptability are more valuable than optimization for one scenario.
options.explanation: Broad skills apply across industries. You're not dependent on any single prediction. Path A concentrates risk on one niche remaining valuable for a decade — the kind of prediction uncertainty makes unreliable.

options.text: You can't decide without knowing which industries will grow.
options.explanation: This is the trap of treating uncertainty like risk. You're waiting for information that doesn't exist. The question is which choice serves you across the widest range of futures.

content.prompt:
Think of a major decision you're facing. Is it under risk (probabilities can be estimated) or uncertainty (probabilities are genuinely unknown)?

Are you treating uncertainty like risk by inventing probabilities? What would change if you used uncertainty tools (robustness, reversibility, optionality, small experiments)?
content.sampleAnswer: Most life decisions are genuinely uncertain. People frequently invent probabilities to make them feel calculable. The exercise reveals whether you've been optimizing for a predicted outcome when you should be building for adaptability across outcomes.

content.body:
## What You Learned

**Risk** has known probabilities. **Uncertainty** has unknown ones. Most important decisions involve uncertainty.

People treat uncertainty like risk by inventing probabilities, creating false confidence in unreliable predictions.

Under uncertainty: optimize for robustness, prefer reversible decisions, maintain optionality, and run small experiments.

When you can't predict the future, stop trying. Prepare for multiple futures instead.

## A Final Note

The risk/uncertainty distinction is the most practically important idea in this course.

Most of what people call "risk management" is actually uncertainty management done badly — using risk tools (probability estimates, expected value) on situations where those tools don't apply.

The next time someone gives you a precise probability for something genuinely uncertain — "There's a 60% chance this deal closes" — ask them where the number came from. If it didn't come from a large reference class of similar events, it's not a probability. It's a feeling with a number attached.

sequences.title: Decision Hygiene
sequences.description: Practical frameworks for making better decisions — putting everything together.

content.body:
You've studied seven biases that distort decisions. This final sequence is about building a practical system for deciding better despite all of them.

**Decision hygiene** (Daniel Kahneman's term) doesn't eliminate bias. It systematically reduces its influence — the way hand hygiene reduces infection without eliminating germs.

The key insight: you cannot debias yourself in the moment. When anchored, you don't feel anchored. When confirming, you feel well-informed. When trapped by sunk costs, you feel responsible.

The solution isn't "try harder to be rational." It's to build processes that compensate for predictable biases before they kick in.

## The Pre-Mortem

A post-mortem asks "What went wrong?" after failure.
A **pre-mortem** asks "Imagine this has already failed. What went wrong?" before you begin.

The pre-mortem flips your brain from advocacy mode ("here's why this succeeds") to diagnostic mode ("here's why this failed").

In advocacy mode, confirmation bias, the planning fallacy, and optimism all push toward believing your plan works.

In diagnostic mode, you're freed to identify risks you'd otherwise suppress. "The launch failed because we didn't test with real users" is easy to say in a pre-mortem but nearly impossible in an excited planning meeting.

The process: before committing, write down independently — before discussion — all the reasons this could fail. Independence prevents anchoring to the first person's answer.

content.prompt:
You're about to sign a lease on an apartment you love. You do a pre-mortem: "It's six months from now and I regret this. Why?"

What's the pre-mortem most likely to surface?

options.text: The same concerns you already had, but more intensely.
options.explanation: The pre-mortem's power isn't intensity — it's coverage. It surfaces what you haven't considered: the commute at rush hour (you visited on a weekend), the noise at night (you visited during the day), the landlord's responsiveness.

options.text: Risks you haven't considered — because the 'imagine failure' framing unlocks concerns optimism was suppressing.
options.explanation: When you love a place, you're in advocacy mode. The pre-mortem shifts to diagnostic mode, giving you permission to notice what optimism was hiding.

options.text: Nothing useful — if you love it, the pre-mortem just generates quibbles.
options.explanation: This is optimism defending itself. "I've thought about this enough" is exactly what a biased decision-maker says.

content.body:
## The Decision Hygiene Checklist

For any significant decision:

**Base Rate Check:** What happens to most people who do this? Is my case really different?

**Sunk Cost Check:** Am I continuing for future value or past investment? Kill question: would I start this today?

**Confirmation Check:** Have I sought the strongest argument against my position?

**Availability Check:** Is my risk sense based on data or vivid examples?

**Anchor Check:** Was my estimate influenced by someone else's number?

**Planning Check:** Inside view or outside view? How long do these actually take?

**Uncertainty Check:** Am I treating genuine uncertainty like calculable risk?

content.prompt:
**This one is designed to fool you.**

A friend says: "I just try to be rational. I think through the logic carefully and trust my judgment."

What's the problem?

options.text: Nothing — careful thinking is exactly what debiasing requires.
options.explanation: Biases don't feel like biases — they feel like careful thinking. When anchored, your estimate feels reasoned. When confirming, your evidence feels thorough. You can't think your way out of biases by thinking harder.

options.text: Cognitive biases operate below awareness. 'Trusting your judgment' is trusting the biased system to evaluate itself.
options.explanation: Biases produce outputs that feel like careful thought. The solution isn't better introspection — it's external processes: checklists, reference classes, pre-mortems, independent estimates.

options.text: You should distrust judgment entirely and rely on data.
options.explanation: This overcorrects. Human judgment has strengths data lacks. The goal isn't replacement — it's support. Use judgment, but supplement it with processes that catch predictable errors.

content.body:
## When to Use What

**Low stakes** (what to eat, which movie): Trust your gut. The cost of being wrong is negligible.

**Medium stakes** (purchases over $500, work projects): Mental checklist run. Check for anchoring, planning fallacy, confirmation bias.

**High stakes** (career change, major investment, relationship decision): Full toolkit. Pre-mortem. Outside view. Independent estimates. Consult a disagreer. Write down your reasoning.

The meta-skill is matching scrutiny to stakes. Most people under-invest in high-stakes decisions and over-invest in low-stakes ones. Invert that.

content.prompt:
Think of the next significant decision you face.

Run through the checklist. For each bias, note whether it might be present. Then do a pre-mortem: imagine the decision has gone wrong in six months. What happened?

Use a real decision you're actually facing.
content.sampleAnswer: Strong responses apply specific biases to a real decision and describe concrete countermeasures. The pre-mortem should surface at least one previously unconsidered risk.

content.body:
## Course Complete

Seven biases:

**Base rate neglect** — ignoring background frequencies.
**Sunk cost fallacy** — continuing because of past investment.
**Confirmation bias** — seeing only what confirms beliefs.
**Availability bias** — judging frequency by memorable examples.
**Anchoring** — being pulled toward the first number.
**Planning fallacy** — underestimating time, cost, and risk.
**Risk vs. uncertainty confusion** — treating unknowable situations as calculable.

One framework: **decision hygiene** — structured processes that catch errors your judgment can't see from the inside.

The goal was never perfect rationality. It was awareness of predictable imperfection — so you can build systems that catch the errors before they become decisions.

Think before you decide. Then check your thinking.
